RESPONSE TO UPND MEDIA DIRECTOR: DEFENDING A BAD LAW IS NOT PATRIOTISM — IT'S POLITICAL MANIPULATION BY REV DR KELVIN MUGALA We strongly reject the false narrative being pushed by UPND Media Director Mark Simuuwe, who claims that the Cyber Crimes Act of 2025 does not infringe on fundamental human rights. This is not only misleading — it is a calculated attempt to sanitize a dangerous law that poses a direct threat to Zambia’s democracy. Let’s set the record straight: 1. The Cyber Law is already being weaponized against critics, opposition leaders, whistleblowers, and citizens. Online harassment laws are being used to criminalize opinion. This is state censorship disguised as cybersecurity. 2. Unanimous support in Parliament does not mean democratic legitimacy. We remind Mr. Simuuwe that Zambians did not elect Parliament to rubber-stamp oppression. The absence of public consultation and the speed of enactment reflect political desperation, not national consensus. 3. This law gives disproportionate power to ZICTA — an institution that is not immune to political influence — opening the door for illegal surveillance and arbitrary arrests. 4. Yes, other countries have cyber laws — but not all cyber laws are created equal. Zambia’s version lacks sufficient checks and balances, clear judicial oversight, and strong constitutional safeguards. 5. Mr. Simuuwe’s suggestion that protestors “lack merit” is a dangerous dismissal of citizens’ rights to dissent and mobilize. That attitude reflects dictatorial arrogance, not democratic leadership. We will not be silenced. We will not be deceived. The truth is simple: this Cyber Law must be withdrawn or radically reformed. It is unconstitutional, unjust, and un-Zambian. LEGAL DISCLAIMER: This response is issued within the scope of freedom of expression as guaranteed under Article 20 of the Constitution of Zambia and Article 19 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR). It is not defamatory, inciteful, or unlawful, but a legitimate critique of public policy and governance.

Why would anyone be against efforts to prevent crime? Isn’t protecting people and digital systems a shared responsibility? Opposing such legislation, without offering viable alternatives, raises questions about one’s true motives. ZAMBIA POLICE SERVICE PRESS STATEMENT ARREST OF JEREMIAH NKUNIKA FOR CYBER HARASSMENT AND HUMILIATION Lusaka,May 14, 2025-The Zambia Police Service wishes to confirm the arrest of one suspect identified as Jeremiah Nkunika, aged 27 years, of an unmarked house on Chovu Chovu Road in Ng'ombe Compound, Lusaka. Brief facts of that matter are that on April 15,2025, the suspect published a false and malicious post on a Facebook page named Icilenkalipa mu Zambia. The suspect has since been detained at Kabwata Police Station and has been officially charged with the offence of Prohibition of Harassment and Humiliation, contrary to Section 22 of the Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act No. 4 of 2025. He is currently awaiting court appearance. We wish to take this opportunity to clarify misleading information that has been published in the Daily Nation Newspaper, Volume 11 Issue 4385, of today May 14,2025, under the headline “Youth Nabbed for Posting Crying Emojis in WhatsApp Group.” The Zambia Police Service wishes to clarify that this is inaccurate and not a reflection of the true nature of the offense committed. We urge all media houses and practitioners to seek official verification from relevant authorities before publishing stories that may misinform the public. Irresponsible reporting has the potential to mislead citizens and erode public trust, and those found wanting should not claim victimization when held accountable under the law. The Zambia Police remains committed to upholding the law and ensuring that digital platforms are not abused for purposes of defamation, cyberbullying, or the spread of false information. Rae Hamoonga POLICE PUBLIC RELATIONS OFFICER

As you may be aware, the Cyber Crimes Bill is now an Act of Parliament which means that it is now enforceable. Kindly bear in mind of the following: 1. *Section 3*. It is an Offense to access someone’s phone or computer (5yrs imprisonment). 2. *Section 4*. It is an Offense to Modify, Delete, obstruct etc someone’s data (5yrs imprisonment) 3. *Section 5*. It is an Offence to communicate “critical information” to someone not authorised (15yrs imprisonment) 4. *Section 6*.!It is an offence to possess “critical information” (15yrs imprisonment) 5. *Section 10*. It is an offense to record private conversations even if you are part of the conversation (2yrs imprisonment) 6. *Section 19*. It is an offence to publish information which is misleading (2yrs imprisonment) 7. *Section 21*.!When you receive summons or police call outs, for Cyber Crimes its an offence to disclose that (5yrs) 8. *Section 22*. Its an offense to be rude, indecent or vulgar with intent to humiliate (2yrs imprisonment) 9. *Section 24*.!It is an act of domestic terrorism to attempt to incite ethnic divisions among the people of the Republic (Imprisonment for life) NOTE: If you are an Admin of any Group etc, be mindful of these crimes and what you post.

In my opinion ,remarks on cyber security crimes Act from some section of society are as dramatic as they are misleading. While it is the right of every citizen, including opposition leaders, to critique government decisions, it is important to ground such criticism in facts rather than political theatrics. First, the Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act was not passed "silently" or in the "dead of night." It followed legislative procedure and was debated in Parliament. The purpose of the law is to modernize Zambia’s legal framework for dealing with cybercrime, cyberbullying, online financial fraud, child pornography, and digital terrorism threats — challenges that every responsible government in the world must address. Second, the claim that the law allows citizens to be imprisoned for 25 years "just for a WhatsApp message" is not only false but also dangerous fearmongering. Like any law, penalties apply only in extreme or criminal cases — for example, in matters involving national security breaches, cyberterrorism, or organized digital crime syndicates. Third, comparisons to rogue states like Sudan or Somalia are not only unfair but also damaging to Zambia’s international image. The U.S. may express concern, but it continues to engage with Zambia as a key democratic partner, even praising Zambia’s regional leadership role in many areas. Fourth, it is ironic that some people speaks of transparency and democratic institutions while simultaneously dismissing the Judiciary, Police, Parliament, and ECZ as “captured.” This kind of blanket discrediting of national institutions undermines democracy far more than any law ever could. Zambians deserve sober, issue-based leadership — not alarmist rhetoric designed to incite fear and score political points. Constructive criticism and engagement are welcome. But let us not weaponize misinformation for political gain. Let the people of Zambia be the ultimate judges — not just of laws, but of those who seek to lead. That's my opinion, what's is yours
